title: Saga Pattern (Choreography)
description: Services publish/subscribe to events for local transactions; avoids coordinator but harder to reason about.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Saga choreography requires event infrastructure (message broker/event bus) plus multiple distributed services. Min ($100): Small-scale deployment with 3-5 services using AWS SNS/SQS ($20-30), EventBridge ($10-20), Lambda functions ($20-30), and DynamoDB for event sourcing ($20-30). Max ($5000): Production enterprise deployment with high-throughput Kafka/MSK cluster ($2000-2500), multiple microservices on ECS/EKS ($1500-2000), RDS for each service database ($800-1000), monitoring/observability stack ($400-500), and data egress costs ($200-300). Costs scale with number of services, event volume, and required durability/retention.
provenance.adoptionCost: Very high complexity pattern requiring: (1) Deep understanding of distributed transactions and eventual consistency (40-60 hours learning/design), (2) Event schema design and versioning strategy (20-30 hours), (3) Implementing compensating transactions for each service (30-50 hours), (4) Building event routing and dead-letter handling (20-30 hours), (5) Complex debugging/tracing across services (20-30 hours), (6) Testing distributed failure scenarios (30-40 hours). Harder to reason about than orchestration-based sagas due to implicit control flow. Requires senior-level distributed systems expertise. Even with AI assistants, debugging event chains and ensuring saga correctness is labor-intensive. Total: 160-240 engineering hours at $50-80/hour blended rate.
provenance.licenseCost: Pattern implementation uses open-source tools: Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ, or cloud-native services (SNS/SQS, EventBridge, Pub/Sub) with pay-as-you-go pricing. No proprietary saga orchestration engine required since choreography is event-driven. Monitoring tools like Jaeger, Prometheus, Grafana are open-source. Commercial options (Confluent Cloud, commercial APM tools) are optional enhancements, not requirements. Core pattern is implementable with $0 licensing costs.

provides.reasoning: Saga choreography provides distributed transaction management across microservices through event-driven coordination, allowing long-running business processes to span multiple services without a central coordinator.
This pattern inherently provides eventual consistency as services react to events asynchronously, with transactions being completed over time rather than atomically.
Services are decoupled through event-driven communication, where services only need to know about events rather than direct dependencies on other services.
Saga choreography IS an event bus pattern — services communicate exclusively through domain events, and this pattern establishes the event-driven coordination topology.

requires.reasoning: Choreography-based sagas require an event bus or message broker for services to publish and subscribe to events that coordinate the distributed workflow.

Event schema governance is critical; using event-schema-registry is more specific and aligns with common tooling like Confluent Schema Registry or AWS Glue Schema Registry to ensure services agree on event formats.
Services must handle duplicate events gracefully since event-driven systems may deliver messages more than once; idempotency ensures saga steps can be safely retried.
Sagas require the ability to rollback/undo operations when failures occur; each service must implement compensating logic to reverse its local transaction.

Critical for debugging and monitoring choreographed workflows across services; without distributed tracing, it's extremely difficult to understand the flow of events and diagnose failures in multi-service sagas.

supports_nfr.reason: Choreography with distributed event coordination across services limits availability to four nines due to cascading failure domains.
Event-driven choreography introduces asynchronous processing overhead with message broker round-trips and service coordination delays.
Tail latency increases due to potential queue backpressure, retry storms, and cascading compensating transaction chains.
Recovery requires each service to restore state from event log and replay compensating transactions across the choreography graph.
Throughput ceiling limited by event broker capacity and the overhead of publishing, routing, and processing choreographed saga events.
Read query throughput bounded by event sourcing materialized view refresh rates and eventual consistency propagation delays.
Write throughput constrained by distributed saga coordination overhead and compensating transaction processing across multiple services.

Saga choreography introduces significant async coordination overhead (event publishing, broker routing, compensating transactions). At very low write throughput, simpler synchronous patterns or orchestration may be more appropriate.
Pattern supports audit logging through event streams but does not mandate it, allowing flexibility in compliance implementation.
HIPAA compliance can be met with encrypted event streams and audit trails but requires additional security controls beyond the pattern itself.
CCPA requirements for data deletion can be implemented via compensating transactions across choreographed services but are not enforced by the pattern.
SOX financial controls are supported through immutable event logs and audit trails inherent to choreography but must be configured explicitly.
Choreography inherently provides eventual consistency with asynchronous event propagation and cannot guarantee read-your-writes semantics.
Strict durability is achievable through persistent event stores and synchronous broker acknowledgments but can be relaxed for performance.

supports_constraints.reason: Saga choreography is a cloud-agnostic architectural pattern implementable on any infrastructure with event messaging capabilities.
Choreography pattern is language-agnostic as services communicate via events with language-independent message formats like JSON or Protobuf.
Pattern applies to backend platforms where distributed transactions span multiple services including APIs, workers, and data pipelines.
Choreography fundamentally requires asynchronous event-driven messaging infrastructure for decentralized service coordination.
Saga choreography is specifically designed to manage distributed transactions across multiple services without a central coordinator.
Real-time streaming platforms like Kafka can serve as the event backbone but simpler message queues also support choreography.
Document stores can be used for service state persistence but are not required for event-driven choreography coordination.
Key-value stores may be used for idempotency tracking or state storage but are not mandatory for saga choreography.
Graph databases are not required for saga choreography though event relationships and saga flows could be modeled as graphs.
Time-series databases are not inherent to choreography but may store event timelines for observability and debugging purposes.
OLTP workloads commonly use sagas for transactional consistency but OLAP analytical workloads can also employ event choreography.
OLAP analytical workloads can use choreography for distributed data transformations though pattern is more common in OLTP scenarios.

event_broker.description: Message broker for event choreography. Kafka offers high throughput and ordering, RabbitMQ provides flexible routing, cloud options offer managed services with different consistency guarantees.

compensation_strategy.description: How to handle rollback when saga fails. Automatic triggers compensating transactions immediately, manual requires operator intervention for critical operations, hybrid uses automatic with manual override capability.

event_ordering.description: Event ordering guarantees. Per-partition ensures order within same entity/key, global ensures total ordering (lower throughput), none allows maximum parallelism but no ordering.

idempotency_handling.description: Whether services must implement idempotent event handlers. Required prevents duplicate processing but adds complexity, optional allows services to choose, none simplifies implementation but risks duplicate effects.

timeout_strategy.description: How to handle service timeouts and failures. Exponential backoff gradually increases retry intervals, fixed retry uses constant intervals, circuit breaker fails fast after threshold, none requires manual intervention.
